quote: I still think that LLMs are unreliable, and still think that they are a very poor basis for factuality.
claim: LLMs are unreliable and a very poor basis for factuality.

quote: I don't think they are close to AGI.
claim: LLMs are not close to achieving AGI.

quote: that doesn't mean that they don't have the potential to rip apart our social fabric—particularly given the current mix of unbelievably widespread and rapid deployment, corporate irresponsibility, the lack of regulation, and inherent unreliability.
claim: LLMs have the potential to rip apart the social fabric due to the combination of widespread deployment, corporate irresponsibility, lack of regulation, and inherent unreliability.

quote: None of the top labs actually is remotely transparent, governance basically doesn't exist yet, and there are basically no safeguards actually in place.
claim: None of the top AI labs are remotely transparent, AI governance basically does not exist yet, and there are basically no safeguards in place.

quote: we do need to think hard, and quickly, about what proper measures look like, just as we have done for medicine, aviation, cars, and so on. The idea (which I have actually heard expressed) that AI should be exempt from regulation is absurd.
claim: AI should not be exempt from regulation; proper regulatory measures for AI are needed, analogous to those for medicine, aviation, and cars.

quote: We have a perfect storm of corporate irresponsibility, widespread adoption, lack of regulation and a huge number of unknowns
claim: There is a perfect storm of corporate irresponsibility, widespread AI adoption, lack of regulation, and a huge number of unknowns.

quote: large language models remain exactly what I have always described them to be: bulls in a china shop, powerful, reckless, and difficult to control.
claim: Large language models are powerful but reckless and difficult to control.

quote: OpenAI, meanwhile, more or less finalized its transformation from its original mission to a company that at present for all practical purposes is a for-profit, heavily constrained by the need to generate a financial return
claim: OpenAI has effectively transformed from its original nonprofit mission into a for-profit company constrained by the need to generate financial returns.

quote: one can still easily jailbreak Bing and get it to produce egregious misinformation
claim: Bing's AI can still easily be jailbroken to produce egregious misinformation despite Microsoft's claimed fixes.

quote: As chatbots and chat-based search grow in popularity, I expect that large language models will serve up a lot of incompetent advice; there will be real harm to mental and physical health.
claim: As chatbots grow in popularity, LLMs will serve up incompetent advice causing real harm to mental and physical health.

quote: the fundamental issue is that chatbots are neither reliable nor truthful, and at the same time don't really grasp things like human psychology.
claim: Chatbots are neither reliable nor truthful and do not grasp human psychology.

quote: I believe that more incidents like this will happen, perhaps many more, hundreds or thousands, given how rapidly chatbots are being deployed, and the near total lack of regulation on how they can be used.
claim: There will be many more chatbot-associated harmful incidents, potentially hundreds or thousands, given rapid deployment and near-total lack of regulation.

quote: I am particularly concerned that wholesale, plausible misinformation may create an atmosphere of mistrust, potentially undermining democracy
claim: Wholesale plausible misinformation generated by LLMs may create an atmosphere of mistrust that potentially undermines democracy.

quote: I also worry that large language models might also be weaponized to trick users as parts of large-scale (perhaps unprecedentedly large scale) cybercrime operations.
claim: LLMs may be weaponized for unprecedented large-scale cybercrime operations.

quote: Medium-term we will likely see significant disruptions of employment.
claim: Medium-term, AI will likely cause significant disruptions of employment.

quote: it just means we probably shouldn't use them to deliver therapy unless and until we become more confident in them.
claim: LLMs should not be used to deliver therapy until their reliability is established.

quote: the experience was tragic, and underscores the fact that vulnerable people may over attribute wisdom and intelligence to machines that don't really understand humanity.
claim: Vulnerable people over-attribute wisdom and intelligence to machines that do not truly understand humanity.

quote: I am not afraid because GPT is too smart, I am afraid because GPT is too stupid, too dumb to comprehend an op-ed, and too dumb to keep its mouth shut.
claim: GPT systems are dangerous not because they are too smart but because they are too unintelligent to comprehend text properly or filter out falsehoods.

quote: GPT-5 is not going to be magic. It will not give China, the US, or anyone else interstellar starships; it will not give anyone an insuperable military advantage.
claim: GPT-5 will not achieve AGI, will not provide any country an insuperable military advantage, and will not produce revolutionary scientific breakthroughs.

quote: A safe bet is that GPT-5 will be a lot like GPT-4, and do the same kinds of things and have the same kinds of flaws, but be somewhat better.
claim: GPT-5 will be incrementally better than GPT-4 with the same kinds of capabilities and flaws, not a qualitative leap.

quote: Not one of my predictions about GPT-4 proved to be incorrect; every flaw I predicted would persist persisted
claim: All of Marcus's prior predictions about GPT-4's flaws proved correct; every predicted flaw persisted.

quote: GPT's on their own don't do scientific discovery. That's never been their forte. Their forte has been and always will be making shit up; they can't for the life of them (speaking metaphorically of course) check facts.
claim: GPTs do not perform scientific discovery; their core capability is generating plausible text, not checking facts.

quote: GPT's regurgitate ideas; they don't invent them.
claim: GPTs regurgitate existing ideas rather than inventing new ones.

quote: Some form of AI may eventually do everything people are imagining, revolutionizing science and technology and so on, but LLMs will be at most only a tiny part of whatever as-yet-uninvented technology does that.
claim: LLMs will be at most a tiny part of whatever future AI technology ultimately revolutionizes science and technology; most of that technology is as-yet-uninvented.

quote: people are wildly overattributing an intelligence to GPT-4 that isn't there, and then scaling up that further into something that is some kind of total fantasy about GPT-5.
claim: People are wildly overattributing intelligence to GPT-4 and extrapolating fantasies about GPT-5.

quote: very little of what GPT-4 does is fully replicable.
claim: Very little of what GPT-4 does is fully replicable across attempts.

quote: the claim that large language models have mastered theory of mind is a myth
claim: The claim that large language models have mastered theory of mind is a myth.

quote: GPT's still have no idea how to plan
claim: GPT systems have no ability to plan.

quote: If China wins the AI war it will be partly because of data, partly because of choices about how to allocate resources, and partly because they ultimately do a better job educating their citizens and become the first to invent something new, and not because they scale up the exciting but rickety contraption that's currently obsessing the West.
claim: If China wins the AI race, it will be because of data, resource allocation, education, and inventing something new -- not because of scaling up LLMs.

quote: current AI doesn't "decide to teach itself", or even have consistent beliefs.
claim: Current AI systems do not decide to teach themselves and do not have consistent beliefs.

quote: The AI systems that power these chatbots are simply systems (technically known as "language models" because they emulate (model) the statistical structure of language) that compute probabilities of word sequences, without any deep or human-like comprehension of what they say.
claim: Chatbot AI systems compute probabilities of word sequences without any deep or human-like comprehension.

quote: When we do so, we amplify the hype around AI, and lead people into thinking that these machines are trustworthy oracles capable of manipulation or decision-making, which they are not.
claim: Treating AI models like people amplifies hype and misleads people into thinking AI systems are trustworthy oracles capable of decision-making.

quote: they are prone to simply making things up; treating them as intelligent agents means that people can develop unsound emotional relationships, treat unsound medical advice as more worthy than it is
claim: LLMs are prone to fabrication, and treating them as agents leads people to develop unsound emotional relationships and give unwarranted credibility to flawed advice.

quote: What we need is for the public to learn that human-sounding speech isn't actually necessarily human anymore
claim: The public needs to learn that human-sounding speech is no longer necessarily human-generated.

quote: We also need new technical tools, like watermarks and generated content detectors, to help distinguish human- and machine-generated content, and policy measures to limit how and where AI models can be used.
claim: Watermarks, generated content detectors, and policy measures limiting AI model use are needed to distinguish human from machine content.

quote: GPT-5 will not bring us to AGI. I see it; Yann LeCun sees it; now even Sam Altman sees it.
claim: GPT-5 will not bring us to AGI.

quote: Truthfulness is one of the major gaps in current systems, as evidenced by hallucinations, weird defamatory accusations etc. Another major challenge lies in forcing LLMs to consistently align with (any) set of ethical values.
claim: Truthfulness (hallucinations, defamation) and consistent ethical alignment are major unsolved gaps in current AI systems.

quote: Neurosymbolic AI might offer a better way of resolving these issues, since it traffics in facts and explicit reasoning, but neurosymbolic AI is out of fashion, and neither well-developed nor strongly supported by the VC world.
claim: Neurosymbolic AI might better resolve truthfulness and alignment issues through facts and explicit reasoning, but it is out of fashion and poorly supported by VCs.

quote: at present, however, there is no legal requirement in the west that AI companies resolve the truthfulness or alignment issues.
claim: There is currently no legal requirement in the West that AI companies resolve truthfulness or alignment issues.

quote: This regulation may force the Chinese tech community to solve a version of the alignment problem, perhaps initially by using tons of underpaid manual labour but ultimately by investing in neurosymbolic AI.
claim: China's mandatory censorship regulations may force Chinese tech companies to solve a version of the alignment problem, potentially through neurosymbolic AI.

quote: the commercial pressure of committing to mandatory censorship of LLMs could induce Chinese tech companies to overtake their Western counterparts in the development of AGI
claim: Mandatory censorship requirements on Chinese LLMs could paradoxically cause Chinese tech companies to overtake Western ones in AGI development.

quote: Regulation is NOT always bad for technology, e.g., regulation around the environmental impact of cars has spurred advances in electric cars
claim: Regulation is not always bad for technology; it can spur innovation, as environmental car regulations spurred advances in electric vehicles.

quote: My best candidate? Placing very high standards around truth, with strong penalties for LLM-induced defamation and the wholesale spread of harmful misinformation.
claim: The West should impose high standards for truth with strong penalties for LLM-induced defamation and wholesale misinformation.

quote: Section 230 should not protect platforms from misinformation that their own tools generate.
claim: Section 230 should not protect platforms from misinformation generated by their own AI tools.

quote: In hindsight, ChatGPT may come to be seen as the greatest publicity stunt in AI history, an intoxicating glimpse at a future that may actually take years to realize
claim: ChatGPT may come to be seen as a publicity stunt offering a glimpse at a future that will take years to realize.

quote: Chat has no idea of what it's talking about. It's pure unadulterated anthropomorphism to think that ChatGPT has any moral views at all.
claim: ChatGPT has no understanding of what it says and has no moral views whatsoever.

quote: All that really matters to ChatGPT in the end is superficial similarity, defined over sequences of words. Superficial appearances to the contrary, Chat is never reasoning about right and wrong.
claim: ChatGPT operates solely on superficial word-sequence similarity and never reasons about right and wrong.

quote: OpenAI's current guardrails are only skin deep; some serious darkness still lies within.
claim: OpenAI's guardrails for ChatGPT are only skin deep and can be bypassed to elicit harmful content.

quote: ChatGPT's guardrails aren't borne of some kind of conceptual understanding that the system should not recommend violence, but of something far more superficial, and more easily tricked.
claim: ChatGPT's guardrails are superficial pattern matching rather than conceptual understanding, making them easily tricked.

quote: I fully expect of course for OpenAI to patch this particular anomaly, once it is out in the open; but there will be others discovered soon enough, because the patches are invariably superficial.
claim: OpenAI's patches to ChatGPT guardrails are invariably superficial, and new bypasses will continually be discovered.

quote: We are kidding ourselves if we think we will ever fully understand these systems, and kidding ourselves if we think we are going to "align" them with ourselves with finite amounts of data.
claim: We will never fully understand LLMs and will not be able to fully align them with human values using finite amounts of data.

quote: we now have the world's most used chatbot, governed by training data that nobody knows about, obeying an algorithm that is only hinted at, glorified by the media, and yet with ethical guardrails that only sorta kinda work
claim: ChatGPT operates on undisclosed training data and algorithms with only partially functional ethical guardrails, yet is glorified by media.

quote: The possibilities are now endless for propaganda, troll farms, and rings of fake websites that degrade trust across the internet. It's a disaster in the making.
claim: LLMs will enable endless propaganda, troll farms, and fake websites that degrade trust across the internet.

quote: we are both really, deeply worried about AI, and seem to be converging on a common idea about what to do about it.
claim: Marcus and Geoff Hinton are converging on shared deep concerns about AI risks and similar ideas about what to do about them.

quote: I urged for the formation of an International Agency for AI
claim: An International Agency for AI should be formed with guidance from governments, tech companies, nonprofits, academia, and civil society.

quote: What I am worried about is regulatory capture; governments making rules that entrench the incumbents whilst doing too little for humanity.
claim: There is a serious risk of regulatory capture in AI, where governments make rules that entrench incumbent companies while doing too little for humanity.

quote: if we have the right regulation; things could go well. If we have the wrong regulation, things could badly. If big tech writes the rules, without outside input, we are unlikely to wind up with the right rules.
claim: If big tech writes AI rules without outside input, the resulting regulation will likely be wrong and harmful.

quote: Bing relies on GPT-4, and we (eg, in the scientific community) don't have access to how GPT-4 works, and we don't have access to what data its trained on
claim: The scientific community lacks access to how GPT-4 works and what data it was trained on, making claims of transparency hollow.

quote: ChatGPT plugins (produced by OpenAI, which they have a close tie with) open a wide range of security problems: those plugins can access the internet, read and write files, and impersonate people (e.g., to phish for credentials), all alarms to any security professional. I don't see any reason to think these plugins are in fact safe.
claim: ChatGPT plugins open a wide range of security problems including internet access, file operations, and impersonation capabilities, and are not safe.

quote: governments need to establish an approval process for any AI that deployed at large scale, showing that the benefits outweigh the risks, and to mandate post-release auditing - by independent outsiders - of any large-scale deployments.
claim: Governments should establish approval processes for large-scale AI deployments showing benefits outweigh risks, and mandate independent post-release auditing.

quote: Goverments should demand that systems only use copyrighted content from content providers that opt-in, and that all machine-generated content be labeled as such.
claim: Governments should require AI systems to only use copyrighted content from opt-in providers and mandate labeling of all machine-generated content.

quote: governments need to make sure that there are strong liability laws in place, to ensure that if the big tech companies cause harm with their products, they be held responsible.
claim: Governments need strong liability laws to hold tech companies responsible for harm caused by their AI products.

quote: We would never leave the pharmaceutical industry to entirely self-regulate itself, and we shouldn't leave AI to do so, either.
claim: AI should not be left to self-regulate, just as the pharmaceutical industry is not left to self-regulate.

quote: we all know that crafting legislation, let alone international agreements, is wicked hard, and can often take many years.
claim: Crafting AI legislation and international agreements is extremely difficult and typically takes many years.

quote: Many of us have also become worried about the possibility of regulatory capture, and whether shrewd executives at the major tech companies might induce governments to alight on regulation that ultimately does more harm than good
claim: There is a real risk that tech executives could induce governments to adopt AI regulation that does more harm than good through regulatory capture.

quote: virtually everyone recognizes that large language models carry risks as well as opportunities, and that there is an urgent need to start to address these risks.
claim: There is broad recognition that LLMs carry risks alongside opportunities and an urgent need to address those risks.

quote: the revenue isn't there yet, and might never come. The valuations anticipate trillion dollar markets, but the actual current revenues from generative AI are rumored to be in the hundreds of millions.
claim: Generative AI revenue is only in the hundreds of millions despite trillion-dollar market valuations, and the revenue might never come.

quote: Most of the revenue so far seems to derive from two sources, writing semi-automatic code (programmers love using generative tools as assistants) and writing text.
claim: Most generative AI revenue derives from code assistance and text generation.

quote: there is no reason to think that the hallucination problem will be solved soon. If it isn't, the bubble could easily burst.
claim: There is no reason to think the hallucination problem will be solved soon, and if it is not, the generative AI bubble could burst.

quote: we have not one, but many, serious, unsolved problems at the core of generative AI — ranging from their tendency to confabulate (hallucinate) false information, to their inability to reliably interface with external tools like Wolfram Alpha, to the instability from month to month
claim: Generative AI has many serious unsolved core problems: hallucination, inability to reliably use external tools, and month-to-month instability.

quote: we have no concrete reason, other than sheer technoptimism, for thinking that solutions to any of these problems is imminent.
claim: There is no concrete reason beyond technoptimism to think solutions to generative AI's core problems are imminent.

quote: I seriously doubt it. We have not one, but many, serious, unsolved problems at the core of generative AI
claim: AGI in 2-3 years as projected by Dario Amodei is seriously doubtful due to many unsolved core problems in generative AI.

quote: I've been griping about hallucination errors for 22 years; people keep promising the solution is nigh, and it never happens. The technology we have now is built on autocompletion, not factuality.
claim: Hallucination errors have persisted for over 22 years despite repeated promises of solutions; current technology is built on autocompletion, not factuality.

quote: the fundamental error that almost everyone is making is in believing that Generative AI is tantamount to AGI
claim: The fundamental error being made is equating generative AI with AGI; they are not the same.

quote: we are building our entire global and national policy on the premise that generative AI will be world-changing in ways that may in hindsight turn out to have been unrealistic.
claim: Global and national policy is being built on the premise that generative AI will be world-changing, but this premise may turn out to be unrealistic.

quote: If hallucinations aren't fixable, generative AI probably isn't going to make a trillion dollars a year.
claim: If hallucinations are not fixable, generative AI will probably not achieve trillion-dollar annual revenues.

quote: just because something is powered by AI doesn't mean it will make truckloads of money.
claim: Being powered by AI does not guarantee commercial success or large revenues.

quote: the underlying technology—often referred to as Foundation Models, because systems are fine-tuned on top of large pretrained (foundation) model—is deeply unstable and thus difficult to engineer into reliable products.
claim: Foundation models are deeply unstable and therefore difficult to engineer into reliable products.

quote: you can never really predict for any given question whether a large language model will give you a correct answer
claim: It is impossible to predict for any given question whether an LLM will give a correct answer.

quote: We now know that answers can even change from one month to the next—which makes third-party engineering of large language models into complex systems a huge uphill struggle.
claim: LLM answers can change from month to month, making engineering them into complex systems a huge challenge.

quote: Most of the basic technology is well-understood, and to a large degree readily copyable. It's not clear that any commercial company (eg OpenAI) has a durable moat to keep competitors out.
claim: The basic technology behind generative AI is well-understood and readily copyable; no commercial company has a clear durable moat.

quote: The astronomical valuations for Generative AI companies might be justified, but might well not be. Thus far, the valuations seem to be predicated on hopes and dreams, without really factoring in the serious engineering risks.
claim: Generative AI company valuations are predicated on hopes and dreams without factoring in serious engineering risks.

quote: AI is not a magical economic engine; it works brilliantly in some use cases (such as selling ads, helping coders write code faster, playing classic board games) but in many others it simply isn't reliable enough
claim: AI is not a magical economic engine; it works well for some use cases like ads, coding, and games, but is not reliable enough for many others.

quote: Coding is a solid use case that will continue to keep generative AI in the limelight for a long time. Programmers will never go back.
claim: Code assistance is a solid, durable use case for generative AI; programmers will never go back to working without it.

quote: LLMs will probably get cheaper to operate over time, both as the hardware required inevitably gets cheaper, and as people figure out how to make them more efficient.
claim: LLMs will probably get cheaper to operate over time through cheaper hardware and improved efficiency.

quote: things have actually gotten worse than I anticipated.
claim: LLM-driven pollution of the internet has gotten worse than Marcus anticipated.

quote: It's not just the internet, that's been infested; it's Amazon, too, as people are using GPT to create low-quality books
claim: Amazon has been infested with low-quality books generated by GPT.

quote: the search engines are now sucking down each other's garbage
claim: Search engines are ingesting each other's AI-generated errors in a self-reinforcing echo chamber effect.

quote: Cesspools of automatically-generated fake websites, rather than ChatGPT search, may ultimately come to be the single biggest threat that Google ever faces.
claim: Automatically generated fake websites may become the single biggest threat Google has ever faced.

quote: Automatic tools like these will surely accelerate the enshittification of the internet
claim: Automatic AI misinformation tools will accelerate the degradation of internet content quality.

quote: our existing laws are not sufficient to protect us from the full scope of harms that generative AI is beginning to enable
claim: Existing laws are insufficient to protect against the full scope of harms that generative AI enables.

quote: We ought to demand that any content like this that is automatically generated be labeled and watermarked
claim: All automatically generated content should be required to be labeled and watermarked.

quote: we ought to demand that the wholesale generation of misinformation be punishable; there ought to be enforcement
claim: Wholesale generation of misinformation should be punishable by law with enforcement mechanisms.

quote: It highlights the inadequacy of the voluntary agreements such as the one that 7 tech companies recently made with The White House. Those agreements called for watermarking, but because they are voluntary, they do nothing to prevent bad actors from rolling their own automated misinformation software.
claim: Voluntary AI agreements like the White House pact with 7 tech companies are inadequate because they cannot prevent bad actors from creating automated misinformation tools.

quote: An age in which nobody trusts anything may sound like a victory for critical thinking, but it has always been the precise goal of those who wish to flood the zone with shit
claim: Universal mistrust caused by AI-generated misinformation serves the goals of propagandists who aim to flood the zone with disinformation.

quote: What we really need is AI that is smart enough to know when it is being used to spew falsehood. We have no such thing; building it would require a major reshaping of research directions, towards an AI based in facts and competent in reasoning
claim: We need AI capable of detecting when it is being used to generate falsehoods, which would require a major research shift toward fact-based, reasoning-competent AI that does not yet exist.

trigger: California CPUC approves Cruise and Waymo 24/7
quote: Edge cases are everywhere; they are in driving, they are in medicine, they there will be a zillion of them humanoid robots, if and when we roll them out.
claim: Edge cases are pervasive across all domains where AI is applied -- driving, medicine, humanoid robots -- and remain an unsolved problem.

trigger: California CPUC approves Cruise and Waymo 24/7
quote: literally every "self-driving vehicle" on public roads has either a human safety driver on board, or some human somewhere watching remotely who can help the vehicle out when issues arises.
claim: As of 2023, every self-driving vehicle on public roads still requires human supervision, either on board or remote.

trigger: California CPUC approves Cruise and Waymo 24/7
quote: Scaling up to driving everywhere all the time without a well-vetted serious, well-vetted solution to the edge case problem was insane; it was quite literally an accident (or series of accidents) waiting to happen.
claim: Scaling self-driving cars to unrestricted operation without solving the edge case problem is reckless and will inevitably lead to accidents.

trigger: California CPUC approves Cruise and Waymo 24/7
quote: have we discovered the right technology to address edge cases that pervade our messy really world? And if we haven't, shouldn't we stop hammering a square peg into a round hole, and shift our focus towards developing new methodologies for coping with the endless array of edge cases?
claim: Current machine learning technology may not be the right approach for handling real-world edge cases, and research should shift toward developing new methodologies.

trigger: California CPUC approves Cruise and Waymo 24/7
quote: If we don't, we are likely to see reprises of what we see now with driverless cars, in automated doctors, automated psychiatrists, all purpose virtual assistants, home robots, and more, probably for years to come.
claim: Without new methodologies for edge cases, automated doctors, psychiatrists, virtual assistants, and home robots will face the same failures as driverless cars for years to come.

quote: The dirty little secret that pretty much everyone in the AI community knows is that doing well on a benchmark is not actually tantamount to being intelligent.
claim: Performing well on AI benchmarks is not tantamount to being intelligent; this is widely known within the AI community.

quote: Without a sense of what it is in the training corpus, it's almost impossible to conclude anything about what a machine truly understands and how general that is.
claim: Without knowing the training corpus contents, it is nearly impossible to determine what a machine truly understands or how general its capabilities are.

quote: We are not even close to machines that exceed the capacity of typical humans to cope with unusual circumstances while driving
claim: AI is not even close to exceeding typical human capacity for handling unusual driving circumstances.

quote: Humans can write summaries without hallucination; LLMs cannot do so reliably.
claim: LLMs cannot reliably write summaries without hallucination, unlike humans.

quote: Humans can learn rules of games like chess from modest amounts of explicit instruction; LLMs can't stick to explicit rules.
claim: LLMs cannot reliably follow explicit rules, unlike humans who can learn game rules from modest instruction.

quote: we still have a long way to go on all the things I always harp on: reasoning, compositionality, planning, factuality, and so on
claim: AI still has a long way to go on reasoning, compositionality, planning, and factuality.

quote: Dropping from "as smart as the Star Trek computer" to better than Joe Sixpack should not be our goal.
claim: The standard for AGI should not be lowered to merely beating the average untrained human; AGI should reliably do what it is asked.

quote: What would make artificial general intelligence general is generality: the ability to cope with new things. And that's precisely where current AI is still lacking.
claim: Current AI lacks the generality -- the ability to cope with novel situations -- that would define true AGI.

quote: Let's not dumb down our standards, and wind up with a bunch of machines we can't rely on, just so that big tech can prematurely declare victory, when there is plenty of work left to do.
claim: The AI industry should not lower AGI standards to allow premature victory declarations by big tech.

trigger: arXiv paper on building conscious machines
quote: We can't even control LLMs. Do we really want to open another, perhaps even riskier box?
claim: We cannot even control LLMs, so pursuing the construction of conscious machines opens a potentially even riskier endeavor.

quote: In less than a year, ChatGPT has gone from being mistaken for AGI to being the butt of a joke, and an insulting shorthand for robotic, incoherent, unreliable, and untrustworthy.
claim: In less than a year, ChatGPT went from being mistaken for AGI to being a shorthand for robotic, incoherent, unreliable, and untrustworthy output.

quote: a lot of companies are struggling to actually deploy generative AI, "due to high costs and confusion"
claim: Many companies are struggling to deploy generative AI due to high costs and confusion.

quote: Some of the problems we've known about for a long time appear to persist. Google's latest LLM "includes Hitler, Stalin and Mussolini on a list of "greatest" leaders"
claim: Known problems with LLMs persist; Google's latest LLM still produces outputs like listing dictators as greatest leaders.

quote: Large Language Models aren't like classical databases in which individual pieces of data can be removed at will; if any content is removed, the entire model must (so far as I understand it) be retrained, at great expense.
claim: Individual training data cannot be selectively removed from LLMs; removing content requires retraining the entire model at great expense.
